title: Why Your Biggest Agency Client Might Not Be Your Most Profitable One
excerpt: For marketing and creative agencies, June fills the project queue with EOFY budget spend. But a full studio and a profitable one are different things, and twelve months of data finally makes the difference visible.
content:
June brings a familiar pattern to marketing and creative agencies across Brisbane, Sydney, and Melbourne. Client annual budgets run out on 30 June. Marketing managers and CMOs who have unspent budget to justify move through their project lists at pace. The studio fills, the brief queue grows, and by mid-June, most agencies are either turning away work or extending hours to meet commitments from multiple clients in the same window.

On the revenue line, June often looks like the strongest month of the year. What it almost never shows is whether the work was profitable.

For most agency owners, the question of profitability at the client level stays unresolved through the year. Revenue comes in by project and by retainer. The studio is mostly occupied. Monthly overhead is covered and something remains. The detail of which specific clients and which work types generate genuine margin, and which quietly absorb it, rarely gets examined until there is a clear twelve months of data to work from. That moment is now.

Why Retainer Clients and Project Clients Have Fundamentally Different Economics

Most agencies carry a mix of retainer relationships and project-by-project engagements. The revenue from each may look comparable in any given month. The margin picture is rarely as similar.

Retainer relationships carry predictable scope. The monthly deliverable set was negotiated once, the team knows the client's brand and processes, and the ratio of studio hours to revenue tends to hold stable across the year. The client has a settled view of value, the agency has a settled view of cost, and the relationship runs with relatively low overhead beyond delivery. Retainer clients are also typically the ones that develop over time, deepening the brief quality and reducing the estimation uncertainty that costs time on project work.

Project-by-project work carries a different structure. Each brief requires scoping, pricing, internal review, and client negotiation before any creative work begins. Timelines are often compressed, particularly in June. The client's expectations of what finished looks like shift between brief and delivery. Revision rounds extend beyond what the original scope anticipated. When those additional hours are absorbed as a goodwill concession rather than captured in the billing, the project margin falls accordingly. By the time the final invoice is settled and actual time is accounted for, the project has often returned considerably less per studio hour than the quoted rate implied.

Knowing the margin split between retainer and project revenue across the full year is one of the most actionable things EOFY data makes possible. Agencies that have run this calculation consistently find that their retainer clients, even when individually smaller by revenue, generate the margins that absorb studio overhead. The project work generates volume, but often at a lower return per hour than the retainer baseline.

The Realisation Gap That Accumulates Through the Year

Realisation rate measures the share of studio time that converts to billed revenue at the target rate. An agency where team members work a year's worth of client-facing hours but invoice for only 80 percent of the equivalent hours has a realisation gap of 20 percent. That gap does not appear as a single line on any report. It accumulates through small adjustments made across dozens of projects: the extra revision round included to protect the relationship, the briefing call that ran long and was not captured, the presentation deck rebuilt when the client's internal direction shifted between brief and delivery. Each individual item is reasonable. The pattern across twelve months is where the cost becomes material.

The clients where realisation runs lowest are often the ones with the highest revenue. Long-standing project clients in particular develop an expectation of flexibility that was reasonable when the relationship was growing and the agency needed the work. By the time that client represents a significant share of the agency's billing, the accumulated goodwill concessions are embedded in a cost structure the current fee level does not support. The revenue figure is large and the margin figure is not, and the two rarely appear side by side in any monthly report.

A

>service line and client profitability review across the agency's full year of project and retainer data maps each relationship by margin contribution rather than revenue. The output ranks clients by what they actually return to the business after all studio time is accounted for. For most agencies running this exercise for the first time, the ranking looks quite different from what the revenue line suggests, and at least one major client sits considerably lower than expected.

What the June Rush Leaves Behind

The June budget flush is a genuine feature of the agency calendar. Clients who want to spend remaining annual budgets before 30 June are motivated to move quickly, and the briefs they bring in can represent welcome revenue at a busy time. But the conditions of June project work are structurally different from how retainer work operates across the rest of the year.

Compressed timelines mean less planning time per deliverable, which typically produces more revision requests than a well-briefed project carried over a proper timeline. Short lead times mean specialist costs, including photography, media placements, and freelance expertise, arrive at rates that reflect urgency rather than planned procurement. And the studio, already stretched by EOFY project volume, means the concession threshold in client negotiations tends to lower when the choice is between absorbing a scope addition and losing the brief entirely.

The result is a June that looks strong on revenue and modest on margin. This is not a management failure. It is a predictable structural pattern that responds well to forward planning. Agencies that know their project realisation rate coming into June can make deliberate decisions about which briefs to accept, which to quote at less margin for flexibility, and where the studio capacity genuinely exists to deliver within the timeline at target margin rather than scrambling to meet a commitment that was priced optimistically.

A

>fractional CFO arrangement builds this tracking into a monthly rhythm rather than an annual discovery exercise, so the realisation gap is visible when it opens rather than when the year closes. For agency owners thinking about their longer-term direction, whether that is growing the business, improving the client mix, or considering an eventual exit, the client-level margin picture from this year's EOFY data is the most useful financial asset the business holds right now. ProfitPulse works with professional services and agency businesses across
>Brisbane and the East Coast to build exactly this picture from the data available at year end. Book a complimentary 45-minute discovery call with ProfitPulse.

faqs.q: What is realisation rate and why does it matter for a creative agency?
faqs.a: Realisation rate is the percentage of total studio time worked that converts to billed revenue at the agency's target rate. If team members collectively work 2,000 client-facing hours across the year but only invoice for the equivalent of 1,600 hours, the realisation rate is 80 percent. The remaining 20 percent represents hours absorbed through scope concessions, unbilled revisions, and work delivered beyond the original brief. Tracking realisation by client and by project type is the most direct way to see where margin is being given away without a deliberate decision to do so.

faqs.q: Why are retainer clients usually more profitable than project clients for agencies?
faqs.a: Retainer relationships carry settled scope, established client expectations, and relatively low commercial overhead once the engagement is running. Project work requires scoping, pricing, and negotiation before any deliverable begins, and scope typically expands between brief and delivery. The effective hourly margin on a well-structured retainer relationship almost always exceeds the margin on project work of the same revenue value, because studio time in a retainer flows more directly to delivery rather than to defining and renegotiating what delivery means. Most agency owners find this pattern clearly when they map clients by margin rather than by revenue.

faqs.q: Why is June often a high-revenue but lower-margin month for marketing agencies?
faqs.a: Client annual budgets expire on 30 June, which drives a surge in project briefs from clients wanting to spend remaining budgets before EOFY. The briefs arrive with compressed timelines, less pricing leverage for the agency, and higher urgency for specialist costs. The studio is typically at full capacity, which means concession thresholds in project negotiations tend to lower. The combination produces strong June revenue alongside project margins that often sit below the agency's annual average. Agencies that know this pattern in advance can plan capacity and pricing discipline before June rather than managing the consequences in July.

faqs.q: How do I calculate whether my agency's clients are actually profitable?
faqs.a: Take the revenue billed to each client across the year and subtract the total studio hours spent on that relationship, multiplied by the average internal cost rate per hour including wages, superannuation, and an allocated share of studio overhead. What remains is the margin contribution per client. Most agencies find their highest-revenue clients are not their highest-margin ones, and that some project-heavy relationships have absorbed considerably more studio time than the billing history reflects. A service line and client profitability review builds this picture systematically using the year's complete data.

faqs.q: What financial metrics should a marketing or creative agency track each month?
faqs.a: Four numbers most directly signal an agency's financial health: realisation rate by client and by project, retainer revenue as a percentage of total billings, team utilisation across billable and non-billable hours, and debtor days on outstanding invoices. Realisation tracks whether studio time is converting to income at the target rate. Retainer percentage signals stability and margin quality. Utilisation shows how capacity is deployed. Debtor days flags where cash is sitting in the billing cycle rather than in the account. Together these give a more complete picture than monthly revenue or project count alone.

faqs.q: How can EOFY data help improve an agency's profitability in the year ahead?
faqs.a: Twelve months of project and retainer data allow a ranking of clients and work types by margin contribution rather than revenue. The pattern typically reveals which retainer relationships are genuinely carrying the business, which project clients generate volume at low realisation, and where scope concessions are most concentrated. Acting on those findings through repricing, scope discipline, or shifting the mix toward higher-margin work types produces a direct improvement in the year ahead. The same data also supports a conversation about whether specific client relationships are priced to reflect the real cost of serving them. A structured financial review at EOFY converts that data from a compliance snapshot into a commercial decision-making tool.

faqs.q: What EBITDA multiple does a marketing or creative agency attract when sold in Australia?
faqs.a: Marketing and creative agencies typically attract three to five times normalised EBITDA, with stronger multiples going to businesses where client relationships are contractual, retainer revenue represents a significant share of billing, work is not entirely dependent on one or two founders, and the client base is diversified. Agencies where the principal holds all significant client relationships personally, or where a small number of clients represent most of the revenue, tend to sit at the lower end of that range. An exit readiness diagnostic identifies which of these factors are most suppressing the multiple and what structural changes would move it before a planned transition.
